<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: prototype-roadmap
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/prototype-roadmap.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/prototype-roadmap.qmd@947b3eb7d
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Prototype Roadmap

A roadmap is dangerous when it sounds like destiny.

The prototype path for the ASI Stack has to do the opposite. It should make
ambition less slippery. It should say which things must exist before stronger
agency is allowed, which artifacts would prove a phase has actually moved, and
which claims remain forbidden even when a demo looks useful.

That makes the roadmap a control surface, not a wish list. It does not say
that the later stages already work. It says that later stages are not entitled
to credibility until the earlier evidence machinery is boring, inspectable, and
able to refuse promotion.

The order matters because prototypes inherit the habits of their first
working version. If the early version treats missing sources, absent replay,
thin tests, or vague authority as acceptable, the later version will automate
that weakness. If the early version records sources, artifacts, gates,
residuals, rollbacks, and non-claims, later autonomy has to grow through those
surfaces.

Progress means reducing the number of things the reader has to take on faith.

## Problem

The ASI Stack needs a build order that does not introduce agency before
auditability.

It is easy to imagine the attractive version first: a planner, memory system,
router, execution layer, benchmark loop, and self-improvement controller all
working together. But if those systems arrive before source boundaries,
artifact continuity, claim ledgers, validation commands, authority stops, and
rollback records, the prototype has recreated the problem the book is trying
to avoid.

The roadmap problem is therefore not lack of ambition. The problem is
sequencing. Every later capability needs to inherit the evidence machinery of
the earlier ones. A planner that cannot preserve source provenance is not ready
to dispatch work. A runtime that cannot replay effects is not ready to close
loops. A replacement system that cannot roll back is not ready to improve
itself.

A useful local trace, dashboard, agent loop, or benchmark result can guide
engineering. It cannot become a public capability claim just because it feels
like progress. A phase has moved only when its acceptance record says what was
run, what passed, what failed, what remains blocked, and what the milestone
does not imply.

## Why Roadmaps Usually Overclaim

Most roadmaps hide dependency direction.

They list features in the order people want them, not in the order that makes
later trust possible. "Autonomous improvement" appears as a destination rather
than the last thing a system should be allowed to touch. "Agentic runtime"
appears as one milestone even though it depends on source ingestion, typed
jobs, permission checks, effect receipts, replay, security boundaries,
benchmarks, and rollback.

The ASI Stack needs the opposite style. Each appealing capability should point
backward to the artifacts that make it legitimate.

The source base already points in that direction. VIEA emphasizes durable
artifacts before execution. Benchmaxxing makes benchmark pressure subordinate
to regression floors and anti-Goodhart checks. Stable Capability Fields make
replacement and rollback prerequisites for governed improvement. VCM requires
source-bound context and adequacy checks before memory-heavy work. PlanForge
requires typed plans before dispatch. Project Theseus adds report-first
machinery, architecture gates, work boards, and self-evolution controls.

External comparators also pressure the order without proving the stack. Risk
management frameworks, extreme-risk evaluation work, formal specification
tools, runtime monitoring, planning systems, software-task benchmarks,
behavioral tests, code-quality metrics, and efficient adaptation methods all
name pieces a serious build path has to respect. They orient the sequence.
They do not say this repository has reproduced those systems or earned their
results.

Without dependency discipline, a roadmap becomes a story about future success.
With dependency discipline, it becomes a way to say no.

## Core Claim

The prototype should grow in staged increments from source matrix and artifact
graph to governed capability replacement.

In the live book, that remains a design rationale at `argument` support.

The source notes support the sequence. They do not prove that any roadmap phase
has been completed, accepted, benchmarked, or used to promote a capability
claim. The existing fixture, prototype phase gate harness, and Lean hooks
validate narrow record-shape, dependency-gate, and phase-route predicates. They
do not turn the roadmap into evidence that the prototype is ready.

The claim is therefore deliberately modest. It says that a governed prototype
should earn stronger agency by first proving that it can preserve sources,
trace artifacts, record claims, run checks, maintain authority boundaries, keep
residuals visible, and refuse unsupported promotion.

The roadmap is a dependency graph for trust.

## The Build Order

The roadmap begins where many AI projects end: with bookkeeping.

First, the project needs a source matrix, source notes, claim ledger, public
source boundaries, and publication validation. This establishes what the system
is allowed to say and where the reader can inspect the basis for that claim.
Without this layer, later automation will produce fluent architecture prose
while silently dropping evidence boundaries.

Second, the project needs artifact graphs, protocol schemas, fixtures, proof
manifests, release records, and audit logs. This turns memory into objects that
can be checked. A claim, test, proof, schema, diagram, report, and release
record should be findable after the run.

Third, intent contracts, planning DAGs, context records, and typed jobs can
arrive. At this stage the system can begin lowering goals into executable
structure, but the work is still bounded by source, context, and authority
records.

Fourth, runtime adapters, permissions, approval surfaces, effect receipts,
replay, and procedural memory can control actual effects. The question becomes
not only whether work can be done, but whether the effect is authorized,
reviewable, reversible, and reusable.

Fifth, routing heads, specialist registries, readiness gates, and benchmark
ratchets can increase capability pressure. They come after the execution and
evidence surfaces because specialists and benchmarks are dangerous when they
are detached from authority, replay, residuals, and rollback.

Only then should the roadmap approach Stable Capability Fields, governed
replacement, policy updates, and bounded self-improvement. Those phases require
evaluator integrity, regression floors, rollback windows, protected invariants,
governance rights, and evidence ledgers.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  S["Source matrix and claim ledger"] --> A["Artifact graph, schemas, fixtures, proofs"]
  A --> P["Intent contracts, plans, context, typed jobs"]
  P --> E["Runtime adapters, approvals, receipts, replay"]
  E --> R["Routing, readiness gates, benchmark ratchets"]
  R --> C["Stable capability fields and replacement"]
  C --> I["Bounded self-improvement"]

  S -. "blocks unsupported prose" .-> Stop1["No source, no claim movement"]
  A -. "blocks lost work" .-> Stop2["No artifact, no acceptance"]
  E -. "blocks unsafe effects" .-> Stop3["No approval or receipt, no default execution"]
  R -. "blocks benchmark theater" .-> Stop4["No residual and floor, no promotion"]
  I -. "blocks authority drift" .-> Stop5["No evaluator independence, no self-improvement"]
```

The diagram is not a completion claim. It is a refusal order. Each later phase
inherits a reason it can be stopped.

## Phase Records

The unit of roadmap progress is the Prototype Phase Record.

A phase record should name:

- the phase id and phase name;
- what the phase unlocks;
- required artifacts;
- acceptance gates;
- blockers;
- validation commands;
- evidence references;
- residuals and phase debt;
- non-claims;
- current status.

That record serves two readers. The builder needs to know what to make next.
The reviewer needs to know what the phase is not allowed to imply.

If a phase proceeds with a missing source, manual workaround, private artifact,
stale validator, incomplete proof, or provisional gate, the phase record should
not hide the gap. It should name the debt, the owner boundary, and the
retirement condition. A blocked phase can still be useful when it says exactly
what would unblock it.

The record also keeps research demos separate from accepted phases. A phase can
be allowed for exploration without being allowed to integrate, promote, or
release. Those are different permissions.

## Invariants

The roadmap has a few simple invariants.

Phases need acceptance criteria. Self-improvement is gated. Roadmap milestones
are not test results. Source-reported readiness is not local reproduction.
Agency does not precede auditability. Provisional evidence cannot unlock
irreversible authority.

These invariants are not slogans. They are release-control rules.

A route scheduler can be explored before it is accepted, but it cannot support
a stronger routing claim without a replayable artifact, baseline, negative
control, adequacy boundary, residual, and evidence transition. A context system
can be prototyped before it is trusted, but it cannot support memory-quality
claims without source-bound packets and adequacy checks. A self-improvement
proposal can be designed before it is enabled, but it cannot become default
behavior without independent evaluation, rollback, and protected invariants.

The live Lean hooks capture a narrow version of the same idea. A roadmap phase
can unlock a dependent phase only when acceptance gates pass, and a phase
milestone cannot promote a claim without evidence artifacts. Those are finite
record-level predicates. They do not prove that any phase is complete.

## Failure Modes

The main failure is prototype theater.

Prototype theater happens when a board, demo, dashboard, generated report, or
agent loop looks like a functioning system while the acceptance gates behind
it are missing. The project appears to be moving because there is visible
activity. The evidence state has not moved.

Another failure is milestone compression. Hard gates collapse into one
impressive label: "agentic runtime," "self-improvement," "memory system," or
"benchmark engine." The label hides source ingestion, schema validation, proof
adequacy, security review, replay, residual custody, and rollback duties.

Operator convenience can weaken the same boundary. A useful tool may tempt the
project to skip approval, receipt, and replay paths. A benchmark may tempt the
project to keep a flattering result without its contamination and regression
context. A local implementation reference may tempt the book to tell a
stronger story than the artifacts justify.

The roadmap should turn those temptations into blocked rows.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a phase table with deliverables, gates,
blockers, commands, evidence references, residuals, non-claims, and status.

The first executable fixture is not a triumphant phase. It tests refusal:

- one accepted non-promoting infrastructure phase;
- one phase allowed for research but not promotion;
- one phase carrying phase debt with a retirement condition;
- one self-improvement phase blocked by absent evaluator independence;
- additional controls for missing artifacts, dependency inversion, promotion
  without an evidence-transition record, phase debt without retirement, and
  missing non-claim boundaries.

`python3 scripts/validate_prototype_phase_gates.py` now checks that surface
with `Phase acceptance checklist` and `Dependency gate review` coverage under
result `prototype_phase_gates_2026_07_02_local`. It proves only fixture
discipline. It does not prove phase completion, deployed build-controller
behavior, benchmark performance, evaluator independence in practice, or no support-state promotion.

## Beyond The State Of The Art

The mature endpoint is a phase-gated build controller for the ASI Stack.

At that endpoint, the roadmap would not be a document someone reads once and
forgets. It would be a dependency graph that helps decide what can be built,
what must remain research-only, what is blocked, what can be integrated, what
can affect claim support, and what should be retired.

A mature controller would give every phase explicit prerequisites, acceptance
gates, commands, required artifacts, evidence references, residuals, unlocks,
phase debt, retirement criteria, and non-claims. It would keep a later phase
from borrowing credibility from an earlier milestone unless the acceptance
record actually exists and the downstream dependency matches the evidence
boundary.

The useful user experience would be blunt. A builder asks what to implement
next. The roadmap returns the smallest allowed slice, the missing evidence,
the commands to run, the artifacts to preserve, the gates that can fail, and
the claims that must not move.

That endpoint remains a target architecture. The book has fixtures, validators,
and finite proof hooks that point toward it. It does not yet have accepted
phase packets proving that the roadmap controller governs a working prototype.

## Summary

The prototype should grow as evidence infrastructure grows: sources, claim
ledgers, artifact graphs, schemas, plans, context, typed jobs, adapters,
routing, readiness, benchmarks, Stable Capability Fields, replacement, and only
then bounded self-improvement.

That order is intentionally conservative. It asks the project to earn agency by
first proving that it can remember sources, preserve artifacts, admit failures,
replay checks, and refuse unsupported promotions.

Every later capability should be able to point to the earlier records that made
it safe to exist. If the pointer is missing, the roadmap should block, narrow,
or mark the capability as research-only.

## Handoff

The roadmap gives the stack a build order. Living Book Methodology gives the
book an operating system for changing without losing its own evidence trail:
manifest order, source queues, claim states, proof targets, validation
commands, reader projections, audio-script paths, changelog entries, and
release boundaries all have to move together.
